# UC-10.12.14 · FIX Protocol Session Health

## Description

FIX session drops or sequence gaps stop trading and breach venue SLAs.

## Value

FIX session drops or sequence gaps stop trading and breach venue SLAs.

## Implementation

Alert on Logout, ResendRequest storms, or heartbeat miss (MsgType=0). Track test vs. prod sessions separately.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: QuickFIX / vendor bridge logs.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype="fix:session"`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Alert on Logout, ResendRequest storms, or heartbeat miss (MsgType=0). Track test vs. prod sessions separately.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="fix:session"
| stats latest(MsgSeqNum) as last_in, latest(ExpectedSeqNum) as expected by SenderCompID, TargetCompID
| eval seq_gap=abs(last_in-expected)
| where seq_gap>0 OR LogonStatus!="LOGGED_ON"
| table SenderCompID, TargetCompID, LogonStatus, seq_gap
```

Understanding this SPL

**FIX Protocol Session Health** — FIX session drops or sequence gaps stop trading and breach venue SLAs.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype="fix:session"`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): QuickFIX / vendor bridge logs. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: trading; **sourcetype**: fix:session. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=trading, sourcetype="fix:session". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by SenderCompID, TargetCompID** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **seq_gap** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where seq_gap>0 OR LogonStatus!="LOGGED_ON"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **FIX Protocol Session Health**): table SenderCompID, TargetCompID, LogonStatus, seq_gap


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search on recent production data, spot-check a handful of results against the source system or operational runbooks (cash operations, EHR, payment gateway, etc.), and adjust thresholds to match your seasonal patterns and test windows so teams are not flooded with noise.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Status grid (sessions), Line chart (heartbeat RTT), Table (sequence gaps).

## SPL

```spl
index=trading sourcetype="fix:session"
| stats latest(MsgSeqNum) as last_in, latest(ExpectedSeqNum) as expected by SenderCompID, TargetCompID
| eval seq_gap=abs(last_in-expected)
| where seq_gap>0 OR LogonStatus!="LOGGED_ON"
| table SenderCompID, TargetCompID, LogonStatus, seq_gap
```

## Visualization

Status grid (sessions), Line chart (heartbeat RTT), Table (sequence gaps).
